message.content:
Task id: T85 (ledgerRefs goals:G4)
Base commit: d1c32f3 (main) — your worktree may be cut from an older HEAD; FIRST run `git reset --hard d1c32f3` inside YOUR worktree (sanctioned base-refresh), then `git switch -c implement/T85` so your commit lands on the task branch.

NOTE: this is a Go repository (wanbond). The check gate is `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...` via `nix develop -c` if go is not on PATH (per AGENTS.md); there is no bun here.

CONTEXT: T83 just landed — internal/bind now has peerState (owning virt/scheduler/sendCodec/paths/outerSeq/fec...), the sharedPathState/peerPathState split, Multipath embedding the primary *peerState, and an endpoint->peer map PLACEHOLDER. Two sibling frontier tasks (T84: per-peer codec/reflector derivation; T86: receive drain across all peers' resequencers) are being implemented CONCURRENTLY on their own branches off the same base — keep your diff MINIMAL and focused on the Send path only, to minimize merge conflicts.

headline: Route Send to a peerState via a per-peer virtual endpoint map

description: Give each peer a distinct virtual endpoint (*udpEndpoint) and build/populate the endpoint->peerState map. Change Send(bufs, ep) to resolve the peerState from ep and use THAT peer's outerSeq, scheduler, fecSend, sendCodec, and path/remote set — instead of reaching the embedded primary via promotion. Preserve the classifier and the m.mu-held path pick + lock-free txBytes accounting (now per-(peer,path)). An unknown endpoint returns the existing wrong-endpoint/no-path error rather than misrouting. Single-peer behavior must remain byte-identical (the primary's virt resolves to the primary peerState).

acceptance: Bind test with two peers each holding a distinct virt endpoint: Send to peer A's endpoint advances ONLY peer A's outerSeq and egresses on A's path set; Send to peer B's endpoint is fully independent; a Send to an unknown endpoint errors and touches no peer. `go test ./internal/bind/...` passes.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.

The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.

BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.

- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).

- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

25	// parity over that, but the 8-byte outer-seq prefix is NOT transmitted — the DATA

171	// fecShardPayload builds the FEC data-shard coded bytes for outer-seq seq over the
172	// opaque inner datagram: seq (8 BE) || inner. The sender codes parity over this; the
173	// receiver reconstitutes the identical bytes from the DATA frame header + payload so
174	// the decoder's shards match what the encoder coded.
175	func fecShardPayload(seq uint64, inner []byte) []byte {
176		b := make([]byte, fecSeqPrefixLen+len(inner))

185	// condition. The inner copy lets the resequencer take ownership without retaining the
186	// larger coded-shard backing array.
187	func splitFECShardPayload(b []byte) (uint64, []byte, error) {

191		seq := binary.BigEndian.Uint64(b[:fecSeqPrefixLen])

24	// udpEndpoint is a conn.Endpoint over a destination AddrPort with an optional
25	// learned source IP. In the multipath Bind it serves as the SINGLE stable
26	// virtual endpoint presented to the engine per peer: the engine holds exactly
27	// one of these while the Bind privately fans out across the per-path sockets
28	// beneath it (see docs/p0-findings.md §3). The engine must never see per-packet
29	// endpoint churn, so every ReceiveFunc returns the very same *udpEndpoint.

52		}
53		return netip.AddrPort{}

15	// FEC datapath integration (T24).
16	//
17	// The fixed-ratio Reed-Solomon engine (internal/fec) is wired into the multipath
18	// datapath as a SEND-side encoder and a RECEIVE-side decoder that composes with the
19	// T18 resequencer, T21 weighted scheduling, and T30 dynamic paths without regressing
20	// any of them. FEC is OFF unless a config carries an [fec] block (NewMultipath's
21	// fecCfg is nil otherwise), in which case the datapath is byte-for-byte the pre-T24
22	// behaviour.

24	// Wire mapping. The FEC data-shard bytes are OuterSeq || inner: the encoder codes
25	// parity over that, but the 8-byte outer-seq prefix is NOT transmitted — the DATA
26	// frame carries the outer-seq in its header and the inner datagram in its payload,
27	// and both sender and receiver reconstitute the shard bytes as
28	// header-seq || frame-payload. This keeps a recovered shard SELF-DESCRIBING (its
29	// reconstructed bytes carry its own outer-seq, so a frame reconstructed from parity
30	// resequences at its ORIGINAL outer-seq even when EVERY data frame of the group was
31	// lost) while adding only one wire byte per DATA frame (the shard index in the DATA
32	// header) and the group cardinality M per PARITY frame. See internal/frame.
33	//

35	// a single deadline-tick goroutine that TryLocks m.mu (never blocking, so it can
36	// never deadlock Close's readersWG.Wait held under m.mu — the tickLivenessFromReceive

38	// concurrent), so it is guarded by its own mutex, disjoint from m.mu and from the
39	// resequencer's — matching the resequencer's lock discipline (never held across a
40	// syscall). Recovered frames are handed to the resequencer exactly like a
41	// natively-received frame, so FEC recovery slots in strictly BEFORE resequencing.

44	// FEC data-shard's coded bytes. It is reconstructed from the DATA frame header on
45	// both ends and never travels the wire (see the wire mapping above).

51	// reconstructed frames must reach the resequencer BEFORE it skips the gap

53	// gap is skipped first and the recovered frame dropped as past the release point. Held

153	// connection-scoped, these carry no path label. The frame overhead the operator cares

155	// groups fill; both are counted only for frames that actually reached the socket, so

171	// fecShardPayload builds the FEC data-shard coded bytes for outer-seq seq over the
172	// opaque inner datagram: seq (8 BE) || inner. The sender codes parity over this; the
173	// receiver reconstitutes the identical bytes from the DATA frame header + payload so
174	// the decoder's shards match what the encoder coded.
175	func fecShardPayload(seq uint64, inner []byte) []byte {
176		b := make([]byte, fecSeqPrefixLen+len(inner))
177		binary.BigEndian.PutUint64(b, seq)
178		copy(b[fecSeqPrefixLen:], inner)
179		return b
180	}
181	
182	// splitFECShardPayload inverts fecShardPayload, returning the outer-seq and a fresh
183	// copy of the inner datagram. It fails fast on a shard too short to hold the prefix,
184	// which would indicate a decoder/encoder mismatch rather than a recoverable
185	// condition. The inner copy lets the resequencer take ownership without retaining the
186	// larger coded-shard backing array.
187	func splitFECShardPayload(b []byte) (uint64, []byte, error) {
188		if len(b) < fecSeqPrefixLen {
189			return 0, nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: recovered FEC shard %d bytes, shorter than the %d-byte outer-seq prefix", len(b), fecSeqPrefixLen)
190		}
191		seq := binary.BigEndian.Uint64(b[:fecSeqPrefixLen])
192		inner := make([]byte, len(b)-fecSeqPrefixLen)
193		copy(inner, b[fecSeqPrefixLen:])
194		return seq, inner, nil
195	}

240		psk  config.Key
241		defs []config.Path
242	
243		// classify maps each outbound datagram to its pacer traffic class from the inner
244		// WireGuard message type, parameterized by the tunnel's Amnezia obfuscation profile
245		// so a WireGuard control frame (handshake/keepalive) is recognised — and pacing-
246		// exempted — under advanced security too, not only in vanilla mode (defect D22). It
247		// is immutable after construction and holds no lock, so Send reads it off m.mu.
248		classify wgClassifier
249	
250		// deferredListen binds a reconciled deferred path's socket (T55 background
251		// reconcile). It is an injection seam: the default pins the source IP (net.ListenUDP,
252		// matching AddPath's runtime bind), and a test overrides it to drive the
253		// deferred→bound transition deterministically — a source_addr "becoming assignable"
254		// — without a real interface address having to appear on the host. Immutable after
255		// construction, never nil.
256		deferredListen func(src netip.Addr, port uint16) (*net.UDPConn, error)
257	
258		mu sync.Mutex
259	
260		// The PRIMARY peer, embedded so the single-peer datapath (Send, the receive drainer,
261		// the probe loop) and the existing single-peer tests reach its fields — virt,
262		// scheduler, reflector, newProber, probers, sendCodec, paths, outerSeq, resequencer,
263		// fecSend/fecRecv — transparently through promotion, keeping behaviour byte-identical
264		// to the pre-split singleton. It is peers[0]; the concentrator's additional peers live
265		// in peers/peersByName. The former process-global resequencer/outerSeq/scheduler now
266		// live on this peerState, NOT on Multipath.
267		*peerState
268	
269		// peers is every bound peer (len 1 on the single-peer edge/hub). The runtime shared-
270		// path add/remove fan-out iterates it so per-(peer,path) state is created/torn-down for
271		// EVERY currently-bound peer (attachSharedPathLocked / RemovePath). peersByName keys
272		// them by peer id/name.
273		peers       []*peerState
274		peersByName map[string]*peerState
275		// peerByEndpoint / peerBySource are the inbound demux lookup maps the concentrator's
276		// shared-socket receive path needs to route a datagram to the owning peer (endpoint or
277		// learned source → peer). They are PLACEHOLDERS today: the single-peer edge/hub needs no
278		// demux (one peer owns every socket), so they stay empty; the concentrator receive path
279		// (a later G4 task) populates and reads them.
280		peerByEndpoint map[netip.AddrPort]*peerState
281		peerBySource   map[netip.Addr]*peerState
282	
283		// shared is the SHARED per-socket path list (the sockets themselves), rebuilt from
284		// m.defs on every Open and mutated by the runtime add/remove. Each bound peer holds a
285		// peerPathState VIEW over a subset of these; on the single-peer edge/hub primary.paths
286		// is index-aligned with shared. len(shared)==0 is the "closed" (no sockets) state.
287		shared []*sharedPathState
288	
289		// deferred holds the configured paths whose well-formed source_addr was not yet
290		// assignable at the last Open (EADDRNOTAVAIL). They are NOT in shared or the
291		// scheduler — the tunnel runs on the paths that bound — but are recorded here,
292		// index-independent of shared, for the T55 background reconcile to retry as their
293		// addresses appear. Rebuilt from scratch on every Open; guarded by m.mu. The deferred-
294		// path machinery is SHARED (per-socket): a promoted deferred path fans its per-(peer,
295		// path) state out to every peer exactly as a runtime AddPath does.
296		deferred []deferredPath
297		// defaultRemote is the fallback per-path remote (the peer's wireguard
298		// endpoint) applied to any path without its own dest_addr. It may be set by
299		// ParseEndpoint BEFORE Open, so it is stored here and applied at Open time.
300		defaultRemote    netip.AddrPort
301		hasDefaultRemote bool
302	
303		// fecCfg / adaptiveCfg are the FEC configuration (T24/T29), shared by every peer; the
304		// per-peer fecSend/fecRecv runtime state Open builds from them lives on peerState. nil
305		// when FEC (respectively adaptive FEC) is disabled — the datapath is then byte-for-byte
306		// the pre-T24 behaviour. See fec.go.
307		fecCfg      *fec.Config
308		adaptiveCfg *adaptivefec.Config
309	
310		// Receive fan-in (T30). To let a path be added at runtime WITHOUT the engine
311		// spawning a new receive goroutine (it only builds its receive goroutines once,
312		// from the ReceiveFuncs Open returns), the per-path socket reads are decoupled
313		// from the engine-facing delivery: the Bind owns one readLoop goroutine PER path
314		// (tracked by readersWG) that reads its socket and feeds the shared resequencer,
315		// and Open returns a SINGLE engine-facing ReceiveFunc that drains the resequencer
316		// in order. A reader pokes deliverSignal after each datagram so the drainer wakes;
317		// recvClosed is closed by Close to release both the drainer and any reader parked
318		// on a delivery. openPort mirrors Open's bind port so a runtime-added path binds
319		// consistently; nextPathID is a monotonic high-water that persists ACROSS Open
320		// spans (a Close->Open never lowers it; only a process restart resets it), so a
321		// surviving path is never renumbered and a freed id is never reused for the
322		// process lifetime, and thus can never collide with the peer's per-path reflector
323		// state.
324		deliverSignal chan struct{}
325		recvClosed    chan struct{}
326		readersWG     sync.WaitGroup
327		openPort      uint16
328		nextPathID    uint16
329	
330		// Receive-path liveness sweep (T39, defect D15). Liveness DOWN-detection normally
331		// rides StartProbeLoop's single wall-clock ticker goroutine (emitProbes → Tick).
332		// Under heavy CPU load — e.g. the concentrator absorbing a saturating forward flood
333		// on 4 vCPU — that timer goroutine can be scheduled with ~1s jitter, delaying a
334		// path-DOWN transition (and thus the reply-direction failover) past the P1 budget.
335		// The per-path receive goroutines, in contrast, are the goroutines the inbound
336		// traffic is ALREADY scheduling, so driving Tick from them re-evaluates liveness
337		// even when the timer goroutine is starved. sweepIntervalNanos is the probe
338		// interval (0 until StartProbeLoop arms it — a bind without the probe transport
339		// never sweeps); lastSweepNanos is the wall-clock high-water throttling the sweep
340		// to at most once per interval so the receive hot path stays cheap.
341		sweepIntervalNanos atomic.Int64
342		lastSweepNanos     atomic.Int64
343	}
344	
345	// compile-time proof that Multipath satisfies the engine's Bind contract.
346	var _ Bind = (*Multipath)(nil)
347	
348	// NewMultipath returns a closed multipath Bind over the configured paths; call
349	// Open to bind the per-path sockets. The PSK keys the outer framing and must be
350	// set (config validation guarantees it). The scheduler is the injected send-side
351	// path-selection policy (T15) whose priority order MUST match the paths slice
352	// order (index 0 = the preferred primary); it is a required collaborator so the
353	// send path is never without a policy.
354	//
355	// probers is the per-path probe initiator that drives on-wire liveness (T13/T37).
356	// When non-nil it MUST hold exactly one *telemetry.Prober per path, in path order,
357	// and those SAME values MUST be the scheduler's PathHealth sources so the liveness
358	// the probe loop measures is the liveness the scheduler selects on. Pass nil to
359	// run the bind without the probe transport (the T12 unit tests drive selection via
360	// sched.AlwaysUp instead). A Reflector is built from the PSK to answer peer probes.
361	//
362	// newProber is the factory the runtime path-add path (AddPath, T30) uses to mint a
363	// prober for a newly-admitted path; it must be non-nil to allow AddPath and MUST be
364	// paired with a non-nil probers (the boot-time set) — a factory without a boot-time
365	// slice would let AddPath append to a nil m.probers, breaking the m.paths/m.probers
366	// alignment invariant and panicking on the next Open at m.probers[i]. Pass nil to
367	// forbid runtime path addition.
368	//
369	// fecCfg is the fixed-ratio Reed-Solomon FEC configuration (T24). Pass nil to run the
370	// datapath with FEC disabled (the pre-T24 behaviour, byte-for-byte); a non-nil,
371	// pre-validated config turns the send-side parity encoder and receive-side recovery
372	// decoder on. It is validated here (fail fast) so an invalid ratio is rejected at
373	// construction rather than at the first Open.
374	// adaptiveCfg is the adaptive-FEC controller configuration (T29). Pass nil for the
375	// fixed-ratio behaviour (T24); a non-nil config REQUIRES a non-nil fecCfg (the
376	// controller resizes the FEC encoder's parity — it is meaningless with the plane off)
377	// and reinterprets fecCfg.ParityShards as the controller's parity ceiling. It is
378	// validated here (fail fast) so a mis-tuned control law is rejected at construction.
379	//
380	// amnezia is the tunnel's AmneziaWG obfuscation profile (config.Amnezia). It
381	// parameterizes the send-path frame-type classifier so a WireGuard control frame is
382	// recognised and pacing-exempted under advanced security — custom magic headers and
383	// handshake junk prefixes — as well as in vanilla mode (defect D22). Pass the zero value
384	// for a vanilla (unobfuscated) tunnel; the classifier then uses the default type words
385	// and no junk prefix. It does NOT need to match the config's validated state — an
386	// all-zero profile is exactly the vanilla classifier.
387	func NewMultipath(paths []config.Path, psk config.Key, scheduler sched.Scheduler, probers []*telemetry.Prober, newProber ProberFactory, fecCfg *fec.Config, adaptiveCfg *adaptivefec.Config, amnezia config.Amnezia) (*Multipath, error) {
388		if len(paths) == 0 {
389			return nil, errors.New("bind: at least one path is required")
390		}
391		if !psk.IsSet() {
392			return nil, errors.New("bind: PSK is required for outer framing")
393		}
394		if len(paths) > 256 {
395			// path-id is a uint8 in the DATA frame header.
396			return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: at most 256 paths supported, got %d", len(paths))
397		}
398		if scheduler == nil {
399			return nil, errors.New("bind: a send scheduler is required")
400		}
401		if newProber != nil && probers == nil {
402			// A runtime-path factory without a boot-time prober slice would let AddPath append
403			// to a nil m.probers, desyncing m.paths from m.probers and panicking on the next
404			// Open at m.probers[i]. The two are paired by construction; enforce it here.
405			return nil, errors.New("bind: newProber requires a non-nil probers (boot-time set)")
406		}
407		if probers != nil && len(probers) != len(paths) {
408			return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: probers must have one entry per path (got %d, want %d)", len(probers), len(paths))
409		}
410		for i, pr := range probers {
411			if pr == nil {
412				return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: prober %d is nil", i)
413			}
414		}
415		if fecCfg != nil {
416			if err := fecCfg.Validate(); err != nil {
417				return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: invalid FEC configuration: %w", err)
418			}
419			if fecCfg.Deadline > maxFECDeadline {
420				// A deadline this large makes every deadline-flushed group's recovery
421				// structurally late: the resequencer skips the gap (resequencerTimeout) before
422				// the parity-derived frames land, so recovery never delivers. Reject it rather
423				// than ship a bond whose FEC silently cannot help (defect #4).
424				return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: fec deadline %s exceeds the max %s (must stay safely below the resequencer's %s per-gap timeout so deadline-flushed recovery lands before the gap is skipped)", fecCfg.Deadline, maxFECDeadline, resequencerTimeout)
425			}
426		}
427		if adaptiveCfg != nil {
428			if fecCfg == nil {
429				return nil, errors.New("bind: adaptive FEC requires a FEC configuration (the controller resizes the FEC encoder's parity)")
430			}
431			if err := adaptiveCfg.Validate(); err != nil {
432				return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: invalid adaptive FEC configuration: %w", err)
433			}
434			// The controller's parity ceiling (MaxParity) is the fixed FEC ParityShards both
435			// ends agree on: the receiver's decoder is built at fecCfg.ParityShards, and the
436			// encoder must never emit more parity than that ceiling (SetParity clamps to it).
437			// A mismatch would let the controller target a parity index the decoder rejects,
438			// so bind the two together at construction rather than trusting the caller.
439			if adaptiveCfg.MaxParity != fecCfg.ParityShards {
440				return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: adaptive FEC parity ceiling (MaxParity=%d) must equal the FEC parity_shards (%d), which the receiver's decoder is built at", adaptiveCfg.MaxParity, fecCfg.ParityShards)
441			}
442			if adaptiveCfg.DataShards != fecCfg.DataShards {
443				return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: adaptive FEC DataShards (%d) must equal the FEC data_shards (%d)", adaptiveCfg.DataShards, fecCfg.DataShards)
444			}
445		}
446		// The single-peer edge/hub constructs EXACTLY ONE peerState (the primary). virt is
447		// created here — NOT per Open — because the concentrator pins its destination once for
448		// the process life and every existing test relies on the virtual-endpoint pointer being
449		// stable across Open/Close/add/remove.
450		primary := &peerState{
451			virt:      &udpEndpoint{},
452			scheduler: scheduler,
453			reflector: telemetry.NewReflector(psk, rand.Reader),
454			newProber: newProber,
455			probers:   probers,
456		}
457		return &Multipath{
458			psk:            psk,
459			defs:           append([]config.Path(nil), paths...),
460			classify:       newWGClassifier(amnezia),
461			deferredListen: defaultDeferredListen,
462			peerState:      primary,
463			peers:          []*peerState{primary},
464			peersByName:    map[string]*peerState{primary.name: primary},
465			peerByEndpoint: map[netip.AddrPort]*peerState{},
466			peerBySource:   map[netip.Addr]*peerState{},
467			fecCfg:         fecCfg,
468			adaptiveCfg:    adaptiveCfg,
469		}, nil
470	}
471	
472	// Open binds one UDP socket per configured path to that path's source address on
473	// port (0 = random per socket), sets a large SO_RCVBUF on each, spawns one
474	// Bind-owned reader per path, and returns a SINGLE engine-facing ReceiveFunc (the
475	// fan-in drainer) plus the first path's bound port. Each path whose config carries
476	// a dest_addr — or, failing that, the peer's wireguard endpoint learned via
477	// ParseEndpoint — starts with a known remote; the rest are learned from inbound
478	// traffic.
479	//
480	// Open is the sole creator of the per-path sockets: the engine's bring-up path
481	// calls Close() first (on the still-unopened bind) and then Open(), so building
482	// the sockets HERE — not in NewMultipath — is what makes that Close→Open sequence
483	// (and every subsequent Down→Up) work. The engine passes the previously-bound

1206	// encodeParityLocked encodes one parity shard as a KindParity frame on the given
1207	// path. Caller holds m.mu (the send Codec is shared and stateful).
1208	func (m *Multipath) encodeParityLocked(par fec.ParityShard, pathID uint8) ([]byte, error) {
1209		return m.sendCodec.Encode(nil, frame.Parity{
1210			FECGroup:    uint32(par.Group),
1211			ParityIndex: uint16(par.Index),
1212			DataCount:   uint8(par.DataCount),
1213			PathID:      pathID,
1214			Payload:     par.Payload,
1215		})
1216	}
1217	
1218	// fecTickLoop drives the FEC encoder's grouping deadline (T24): a partially-filled
1219	// group whose size threshold has not been reached is closed on time so its parity is
1220	// emitted rather than stranded until the next data frame. It ticks at the configured
1221	// deadline period and exits on recvClosed. It is tracked by readersWG so Close waits
1222	// for it. Like tickLivenessFromReceive it only ever TryLocks m.mu, so it can never
1223	// deadlock Close's readersWG.Wait (which runs while Close holds m.mu).
1224	func (m *Multipath) fecTickLoop(period time.Duration, closed <-chan struct{}) {
1225		defer m.readersWG.Done()
1226		ticker := time.NewTicker(period)
1227		defer ticker.Stop()
1228		for {
1229			select {
1230			case <-closed:
1231				return
1232			case <-ticker.C:
1233				m.fecFlushDeadline()
1234			}
1235		}
1236	}
1237	
1238	// fecFlushDeadline closes any FEC group whose grouping deadline has elapsed and emits
1239	// its parity on a scheduler-chosen path. It TryLocks m.mu: when the lock is contended
1240	// (a concurrent Send/Close/AddPath) it simply skips this tick — the next tick, or the
1241	// next size-triggered close, still emits the group's parity, and skipping preserves
1242	// Close's invariant that no readersWG goroutine blocks on m.mu. Framing runs under the
1243	// lock (the shared stateful send Codec); the socket writes run without it, mirroring
1244	// Send.
1245	func (m *Multipath) fecFlushDeadline() {
1246		if !m.mu.TryLock() {
1247			return
1248		}
1249		if m.fecSend == nil || len(m.paths) == 0 {
1250			m.mu.Unlock()
1251			return
1252		}
1253		// Adaptive drive (T29): fold a fresh loss sample into the controller and retarget the
1254		// encoder's per-group parity BEFORE flushing, so a group closed by this tick already
1255		// carries the current target. It is a no-op in fixed mode and self-throttles to the
1256		// probe cadence. Runs under the same m.mu the flush holds — the single serialized FEC
1257		// locus — so the controller (a state machine) is never touched concurrently.
1258		m.driveAdaptiveControllerLocked()
1259		parity, err := m.fecSend.enc.Tick()
1260		if err != nil || len(parity) == 0 {
1261			m.mu.Unlock()
1262			return
1263		}
1264		// Route the parity like data through the scheduler (a first-integration choice; see
1265		// Send). A shed/no-path verdict drops this group's parity — a degraded path is
1266		// exactly when the datapath is under pressure, and a stranded partial group's parity
1267		// is best-effort — so the group simply goes unprotected rather than blocking. FEC
1268		// parity is bulk redundancy, so it is paced as ClassData (defect D22): only WireGuard
1269		// control frames earn the pacing exemption.
1270		idx := m.scheduler.Pick(sched.ClassData)
1271		if idx < 0 || idx >= len(m.paths) {
1272			m.mu.Unlock()
1273			return
1274		}
1275		ps := m.paths[idx]
1276		remote, ok := ps.getRemote()
